# Practical Systems — Episode
## On-Call That Doesn't Break People
Host: Ethan
Guest: Priya Nair
#fixture-v3: failure_modes=asr_garble,recurring_guest,position_arc_multi
#fixture-v3: voice=en-IN host_voice=en-US

[00:00]
Ethan: Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about on call rotation, and I'm joined by Priya Nair, principal SRE at a payments platform. Priya Nair, thanks for being here.
Priya Nair: Thanks, Ethan. Excited for this one — incident response and on-call design is something I've been thinking about a lot.

Ethan: This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first. Get started at linear.com/podcast.
Priya Nair: I'm a believer in centralizing the security function. One team, one source of truth, one neck to wring.
Ethan: That's a real shift. Worth pulling on.

[03:30]
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya Nair: Let me give you the version I actually believe. A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya Nair: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Alerting should be action-oriented. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. Where do reliability and on call rotation intersect?
Priya Nair: Here's how I think about it. Error budgets work when they change behavior. We've seen this play out at teams using Sentry. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Priya Nair: Sentry is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Ethan: I want to dig into that. What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya Nair: Yeah, so — PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[12:00]
Ethan: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by PagerDuty. PagerDuty turns noisy alerts into structured incident response. Visit pagerduty.com/podcast for a free trial.
Ethan: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Ethan: That's a useful framing. Where do reliability and on call rotation intersect?
Priya Nair: Honestly, A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya Nair: It comes down to this: Alerting should be action-oriented. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Ethan: That's a useful framing. What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya Nair: It comes down to this: Error budgets work when they change behavior. We've seen this play out at teams using Sentry. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Ethan: Say more about that.
Priya Nair: Outside of Sentry-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya Nair: Let me give you the version I actually believe. PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.

[22:00]
Ethan: Let's pivot — systems thinking is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Priya Nair: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around systems thinking cross-cut everything we've covered.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya Nair: Here's how I think about it. A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Ethan: Let's start there. What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Priya Nair: Yeah, so — Alerting should be action-oriented. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. Where do reliability and on call rotation intersect?
Priya Nair: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Error budgets work when they change behavior. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Ethan: That maps to what I've seen.
Priya Nair: And when teams adopt Sentry in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: That's a useful framing. Where do reliability and on call rotation intersect?
Priya Nair: Honestly, PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[28:00]
Ethan: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Priya Nair: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Ethan: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at Sentry. Sentry catches the errors your tests didn't. Check out sentry.com.

Ethan: Priya Nair, thanks for the conversation.
Priya Nair: Thanks, Ethan.
Ethan: That's it for today's episode of Practical Systems. See you next time.
